Now, the code steps in C++:

In the arrangeWords function:

- Split the input text into words, converting each to lowercase.

Now, code steps:

class Solution {
public:
    string arrangeWords(string text) {
        // Split into words
        istringstream iss(text);
        vector<string> words;
        string word;
        while (iss >> word) {
            words.push_back(word);
        }
        // Convert each word to lowercase
        for (auto& w : words) {
            transform(w.begin(), w.end(), w.begin(), ::tolower);
        }
        // Create indices vector
        vector<int> indices(words.size());
        for (int i = 0; i < words.size(); ++i) {
            indices[i] = i;
        }
        // Sort indices based on word lengths and original positions
        sort(indices.begin(), indices.end(), [&](int i, int j){
            if (words[i].size() != words[j].size()) {
                return words[i].size() < words[j].size();
            }
            return i < j;
        });
        // Build sorted words
        vector<string> sorted_words;
        for (int idx : indices) {
            sorted_words.push_back(words[idx]);
        }
        // Capitalize first word's first letter
        if (!sorted_words.empty()) {
            sorted_words[0][0] = toupper(sorted_words[0][0]);
        }
        // Join the words
        ostringstream oss;
        for (int i = 0; i < sorted_words.size(); ++i) {
            if (i > 0) {
                oss << " ";
            }
            oss << sorted_words[i];
        }
        return oss.str();
    }
};

Joining the words: O(total length of all words) = O(n).

Space complexity: O(m) for the indices vector and the sorted_words vector, plus O(n) for the words. So overall O(n).

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    string arrangeWords(string text) {
        istringstream iss(text);
        vector<string> words;
        string word;
        while (iss >> word) {
            words.push_back(word);
        }
        for (auto& w : words) {
            transform(w.begin(), w.end(), w.begin(), ::tolower);
        }
        vector<int> indices(words.size());
        for (int i = 0; i < words.size(); ++i) {
            indices[i] = i;
        }
        sort(indices.begin(), indices.end(), [&](int i, int j) {
            if (words[i].size() != words[j].size()) {
                return words[i].size() < words[j].size();
            }
            return i < j;
        });
        vector<string> sorted_words;
        for (int idx : indices) {
            sorted_words.push_back(words[idx]);
        }
        if (!sorted_words.empty()) {
            sorted_words[0][0] = toupper(sorted_words[0][0]);
        }
        ostringstream oss;
        for (int i = 0; i < sorted_words.size(); ++i) {
            if (i > 0) oss << " ";
            oss << sorted_words[i];
        }
        return oss.str();
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n log n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution splits the input into words, converts them to lowercase, and sorts them by length while preserving original order for ties using indices. After sorting, the first word is capitalized and the rest are joined. The approach is efficient with O(n log n) time due to sorting and linear space for storing words and indices.
